title: Born on Section 7
summary: Interviewed by Emilye Crosby at the Library of Congress in December 2015, Juadine Henderson opens with her birth in Batesville, Panola County, Mississippi, to farmers Leora Morris and Hugo Henderson, her father's death when she was four or five, her mother's remarriage when she was ten, and the family's move to eighty acres of land owned by groups of brothers and cousins that everyone called Section 7.

title: Walk the Glasses Out Front
summary: Henderson recalls washing dishes at age eight in Brown's Cafe where her mother worked, being told to carry a tray of glasses out front to see the white diners, her sisters and a brother who has since died, and starting school at age three because her grandmother taught at the church-run Olive Ray School where she learned to read and write early.

title: I Cried for His Parents
topic: Emmett Till and the Color Line
summary: She describes riding a farm truck to school while white children passed on a bus, playing with poor white neighbors who had to watch for their father's car, and at age eight reading the magazine coverage of the Emmett Till murder her mother gave her, weeping for his parents and knowing something was seriously wrong.

title: You Must Never Say That Again
topic: The NAACP Card and the Church
summary: Henderson tells of finding her mother's NAACP card and being warned never to say the name again, the hushed meetings in their two-room former store, and Sunday sermons at West Camp Missionary Baptist Church where Reverend Middleton taught Black history and the congregation discussed Little Rock and voter registration.

title: Walking Down the Road With Their Guns
summary: She recounts the NAACP-active men of the county, C.J. Williams, Robert Miles, and Willie Kirkendall, and how when a sheriff ordered Kirkendall to beat his daughter falsely accused of stealing and Kirkendall refused, the men of the neighborhood walked down the road with guns on their shoulders to climb into the trees and trucks and protect the family, who then moved to Indiana.

title: There Was a Stranger There
summary: Henderson describes meeting SNCC's Frank Smith at her cousin's house at Thanksgiving 1962 when she was thirteen, riding with him to point out the house of a relative of one of Emmett Till's killers, telling her mother that Black people in Batesville could change the whole system if they voted, and beginning to organize her friends.

title: Freedom in the Air
summary: She tells how the young people met secretly at Concord Missionary Baptist Church with a record player, learned freedom songs from the album Freedom in the Air featuring Bernice Johnson Reagon and the Albany Movement, putting the word freedom where Jesus had been, and how she once played Oh Freedom instead of Amazing Grace and survived the elders' shock.

title: Headed to Greenwood
summary: Henderson recounts hiding a packed bag under the bed to attend a voter registration workshop in Greenwood, her mother consulting an uncle who let her go because her older cousin Jimmy was going, the June 1963 trip with cousins and friends including John Hardy, and arriving at the hushed, serious Greenwood SNCC office to stay with a woman called Mama Hackman.

title: Medgar Evers Was Killed Last Night
summary: She describes her first mass meeting, learning the next morning that Medgar Evers had been killed in Jackson and the boy called Freedom delivering the news, canvassing the neighborhood with leaflets, the largest gathering she had ever seen, and meeting John O'Neal, Willie Peacock, and James Peacock at voter registration workshops.

title: Connecting the Dots
summary: Henderson tells of practicing the long voter registration forms and interpreting a section of the state constitution, learning only two Black people in the county were registered, and describing Panola County where Northern landowners held their own land with little sharecropping apart from the Macmillan plantation, in a town that was fifty-four percent Black with no Black voters.

title: We Found Out Later
summary: She recalls that while she was in Greenwood, June Johnson, Fannie Lou Hamer, and Euvester Simpson came up missing and Lawrence Guyot was sent to look for them, that she eavesdropped on the discussions but only learned later from another group of Batesville kids what had happened, and that the news did not register as fear because the women were still strangers to her.

part: The Summer Project Comes to Batesville
title: I Think Those People Hypnotized You
summary: Henderson describes returning to harass her mother and stepfather about registering to vote, her mother saying the movement people had hypnotized her, the small neighborhood meetings, and her resentment that when the 1964 Summer Project brought crowds of outsiders to town after she had been begging people to act for a year, though it broadened the movement countywide.

title: She Hit Me With the Fly Swatter
summary: She recounts being driven home late by Jeff Cowan, a young white Harvard volunteer, and her mother hitting her with a fly swatter for riding around with white children, then describes her stepfather memorizing and reciting Section 30 of the Mississippi Constitution all night, with a little drink to help, so that both parents passed the registration test in 1964.

title: Everybody Was From West Camp
summary: Henderson names the older Panola movement people, Robert Miles, C.J. and Jasper Williams who had attended Piney Woods boarding school, and the Robinson family, most of them West Camp landowners who traced their ancestry to Guinea, and notes that her mother spent three years in eighth grade because there were no high schools for Black children until the 1950s.

title: I'm Coming Out of the Kitchen With a Knife
summary: She describes the young people moving to direct action in 1964, her mother quitting her restaurant job and vowing to come out with a knife if anyone touched her daughter, and a sit-in at that same restaurant where the waitress Miss Lil nervously refused to serve them and they sat in the booths after practicing nonviolent workshops.

title: Slinging Us Across the Floor
summary: Henderson recounts white farmers in brown cotton gloves throwing the demonstrators into the street and banging heads together, a second restaurant locking its doors as pool-hall men came out with cues, and the deputies finally marching the singing students to jail where the sheriff booked them but refused to lock the cell, releasing those under eighteen at five o'clock.

title: Take Some Ice Water for the Kids
summary: She tells of being picked up again by the sheriff while carrying ice water to jailed friends, the whole community turning out for the over-eighteen trials, Robert Miles decking a man who tried to hit white volunteer Chris Williams, and the juvenile court judge warning that continued protest would leave them with a criminal record when freedom came.

title: You're Moving Out of That Room
summary: Henderson describes a deputy stepping into their house one Saturday morning, saying wrong house and leaving, her stepfather concluding the man had come to locate her room for a night attack and immediately moving her and loading a shotgun, and her high school principal quietly offering help after a gun was fired near the jail.

title: Almost Every Woman Petted Her Purse
summary: She recounts a voter registration meeting at West Camp where, told strangers were inquiring outside, almost every woman patted the pistol in her purse and the response was carry on, that the women including her mother were good shots who carried even to church without the men knowing, and that her grandfather tried but failed to teach her to shoot after she turned eighteen.

title: That's Where I Have to Be
summary: Henderson describes winning an academic scholarship to Mississippi Industrial College, then hearing that a thousand farm workers had walked off Delta plantations and deciding at eighteen to work in the movement full time, telling her disbelieving mother, taking ten dollars slipped by her older sister, and catching a ride to Jackson with project head Lou Grant.

title: Stay With Us
topic: Mount Beulah and a Jackson March
summary: She tells of arriving at the Mount Beulah conference center, fleeing a room with no locks when a man got into the next bed and finding three older women from Batesville who took her in, spending her last money on a dress, and going to the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party office in Jackson to canvass for a demonstration while pretending she knew the city.

title: Passing the Trench Coat Around
summary: Henderson recounts being arrested within an hour at the Jackson demonstration and held two weeks at the Fairgrounds with about a thousand women, sharing a single trench coat to bathe, washing clothes with ivory soap, stacking mattresses at five in the morning, the boiling wash tanks, and the authorities separating the white women into the jail.

title: She Steps Out and Starts Praying
summary: She describes a Fairgrounds standoff when a woman refused to give up her mattress and took an officer's billy club, the police facing the cursing women until Mrs. Thelma Glover of Batesville walked into the line praying and swinging her arms until they backed out, and her bail arriving with a change of clothes sent by a woman whose sister was a Black police officer.

title: Just What I Needed
summary: Henderson tells of reuniting with Hollis Watkins, going to the scary town of McComb, meeting Curtis Hayes who was assembling Mississippi young people to organize the Delta, traveling to Cleveland near the Mississippi Freedom Labor Union work, and joining the mostly male Freedom Corps paid ten dollars a week by the Delta Ministry through the National Council of Churches.

title: Owen Was Like a Mother Hen
summary: She recounts Owen Brooks of the Delta Ministry threatening to call her mother when she stayed out all night at a juke joint and placing her with the Washington family, then her move to Rosedale to live with local leader Frank Davis alongside Morton Thomas of Harvard and Billy Kochiyama, and names the local Freedom Corps members including Isaac Foster and Joe Harrison.

title: I'm Playing First Base
topic: Holding Her Own and the Chase
summary: Henderson describes telling Benjamin Brown she would play first base whether or not he liked it, recruiting women like Helen Williams who could hang and friends who could not, and the dangerous canvassing on plantations where a lookout in the van beeped the horn when the owner's dust appeared so everyone could run, sometimes returning to find families had fled overnight.

title: Three Dollars a Day
summary: She explains the Mississippi Freedom Labor Union effort to organize farm workers earning thirty cents an hour for ten-hour days to walk off for higher pay, the company-store debts that trapped them, her tiny freedom school teaching dropouts to write their names, and Billy Kochiyama, whose family had been interned in World War II, telling her she was African American.

title: It Was My First Plane Ride
summary: Henderson recounts the Freedom Labor Union walkouts that left people without support and the tent camp at the Greenville Air Force Base, then being sent on her first plane ride, terrified, to speak at white church groups in Minnesota, Pennsylvania, and Illinois with Reverend Robert Beach in late 1965, where congregations stared at her natural hair.

title: A Wonder We Don't See You Hung
summary: She tells of a Pittsburgh woman warning that Black people did not go out to Mount Lebanon after dark, her sister in Chicago straightening her hair and dressing her in a suit and heels for a church talk, and her decision to keep her hair natural after seeing Gwen Gillian and an Odetta album cover, against her sister's wishes.

title: It's Time to Move
summary: Henderson describes the Freedom Corps breaking up as the politics of the movement shifted in early 1966, leaving a Bolivar County family's home where a mouse ran across her pillow, and moving to Jackson to live in the Patio Apartments with movement women including Gracie Hartthorn, Joyce Brown, and Marjorie Hyatt who worked for the MFDP.

title: You Need to Go See Dr. King
topic: Strike City and a Push to College
summary: She recounts working in Frank Smith's neighborhood development office making bricks for Strike City, the tent town where people went after being kicked off plantations, meeting Jean Wheeler who was married to Frank and became a big sister, and a recruiter from Bishop College in Texas drawing the movement young people who had finished high school.

title: Look, She Washed Her Hair
topic: An Afro at a Baptist College
summary: Henderson describes the Bishop College recruiter's repent-and-be-saved car, students staring at her short afro as though she had simply washed her hair, being assigned to the laundry room because she would touch no one's hair, being fired after a hairdryer roller burned and smoked, and a librarian comparing her to an African woman.

title: Can You Do My Hair Like That
topic: A Hair Movement and Sunday Visits
summary: She tells how classmates soon asked her to cut their hair natural, how Ebony's 1966 all-natural cover pleased her mother and made afros acceptable, and how at the strict Baptist campus she skipped chapel to play guitar and spent Sundays visiting Lafayette, a federal prisoner at Seagoville she reached by signing out for a Quaker meeting.

title: Put Your Clothes On, We're Going to See Dr. King
topic: King's Visit and a New Push
summary: Henderson recounts leaving Bishop in 1968, her depression and her uncle marching her out to see Dr. King speak in Batesville before his death, her surprise that young people were with him, and her friend Tate arriving to organize the Poor People's Campaign as King was assassinated shortly after.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

title: And Then You Can Go Home
summary: She describes going to the Lorraine Motel in Memphis, helping get people on buses to Washington while insisting she would not go, then being passed from Selma to Montgomery to Birmingham each time told she could go home afterward, ending up in Washington with the Mule Train, and meeting reporters Gerald Fraser, Thomas Johnson, Robert Maynard, and Earl Caldwell whose work fascinated her.

title: Living Out Here in Mud
summary: Henderson contrasts the poor people living in the muddy tents of Resurrection City on the National Mall with the SCLC staff who had warm rooms at the Pitts Motor Inn, recounts spotting Charlie Cobb on 14th Street after the riots, and how Frank Smith finally told her to get her bags and pulled her out of the camp and the campaign.

title: Every Book in the World About Black People
summary: She tells of becoming a student of Frank Smith's at the Institute for Policy Studies among fellows like Ivanhoe Donaldson, then taking a job at the Drum and Spear Bookstore founded by Charlie Cobb and Afro-American Resources, the largest Black bookstore she had seen, its link to the Center for Black Education, and the Sunday radio program Sauti ya Watoto.

title: They Tear Gassed the Bookstore
summary: Henderson recounts the police tear-gassing the store while historian Lerone Bennett Jr. signed copies of Before the Mayflower, Ralph Featherstone becoming manager and making the store more political, and the store moving to a bigger location only for Featherstone to be killed by a car bomb with Che Payne on March 10, 1970, with no counseling offered.

title: You Actually Put On Clothes
topic: Office Work and the West Indies
summary: She describes leaving the bookstore for a receptionist job at the Black Child Development Institute where she hid that she could not type by working at night, meeting Florence Tate who became a big sister, becoming a program analyst, and a recuperative trip with Judy Richardson and Mimi through Puerto Rico, Haiti, and Trinidad, including a Haitian guide who took her to a vodou ceremony whose drumming terrified her into promising never to return.

title: A Big Boom on the Third
topic: The July Picnic and the Drums
summary: She describes the two-day picnic every July third and fourth in northern Panola County where old men heated drumheads over a fire and only the men danced, including her embarrassing stepfather, her later discovery online that the Smithsonian had recorded the drummers in the 1940s and traced their rhythms to Mali, the customs of saluting the drums and serving goat that Alan Lomax noted elsewhere but her community did not follow, and Othar Turner, who came after Sid Hemphill, and the wish to recover the recordings for younger people.

title: Not to Bring Peace But a Sword
summary: She returns to playing Oh Freedom in church and reflects that she stopped playing piano and going to church after she turned eighteen, the deacon who locked the church and went house to house warning parents yet later sheltered volunteers, and West Camp opening its doors when Concord locked hers.

title: A Movement Town
summary: Henderson contrasts active Greenwood with quieter Batesville, recalls meeting Freddie Greene and reconnecting over the years, and describes Robert Miles as a strong farmer and community man who once checked on the children playing church, alongside the outspoken teacher Mrs. Glover who stood praying between the police and the demonstrators.

title: They Watched Them
summary: She describes the active Panola women like Pearl and Virgil Robinson who took in civil rights workers and got people to the polls without speaking publicly, the Thomas Sundry where people gathered, the shock and curiosity at white volunteers like Penny Patch and Claire O'Connor walking the neighborhood, and her mother keeping her separate from the volunteers who would leave.

title: He Left His Car in Town
summary: Henderson recounts serious family trouble before the 1960s when an uncle jumped by white men in Pope led to a shooting that left one dead and one paralyzed, how one uncle took responsibility by leaving his car as a decoy and turning himself in when the family was threatened, his conviction and electrocution around 1952, and her father's mysterious death eleven months later.

title: It Was Performance Art
summary: She describes learning of her father's death from her uncle while pretending not to already know, treating the funeral as performance art and watching her older cousin to know when to cry, and recalls relatives suspecting she joined the movement because of the family trouble and asking where her daddy was without knowing they had executed the wrong Henderson.

title: Only I Know, and I Will Never Tell
summary: Henderson tells of her jailed relative refusing to let her bring ice cream so she would not remember him that way, moving to California in 1979 partly to learn from the surviving uncle what happened that night, his answer that only he knew and would never tell, and his widow later revealing that he had actually done the killing while his brother took the blame.

title: He Owned Thirteen People
summary: She recounts learning that her great-great-grandfather Duncan C. Henderson was a slaveholder who lived with his Black family and founded the Henderson school in Panola County, finding him on the slave schedule owning thirteen unnamed people, and a cousin helping recover the name of an enslaved ancestor, Classy.

title: I Decided I Wanted to Be a Doctor
summary: Henderson describes returning from the West Indies and Florence Tate keeping her working, brief stints studying premed at George Washington University and jobs at the Children's Defense Fund and the Interreligious Foundation, then the National Urban Coalition as a public relations writer, before reading of Columbia's Michele Clark Fellowship for minority journalists and being accepted in 1974.

title: A Sneaky Shy
summary: She recounts being too shy to ask a gubernatorial candidate a single question, Earl Caldwell drilling her on note-taking at the weekly paper Deadline, writing a story she liked about an escaped North Carolina prisoner, covering the Newport Jazz Festival, and starting her first newspaper job at the Daily Argus in Mount Vernon with a front-page story she did for twenty-eight years in journalism.

title: The Movement Is Us
summary: Henderson reflects that the movement and her strong family shaped her, recalls her grandmother teaching and her grandfather the farmer-healer who wanted her to be a lawyer, says she stays in touch with Charlie Cobb and Frank Smith, and stresses that young people once stood up to power as they do now, and that she left the church at eighteen.

title: Marched Us Off to Parchman
summary: She recounts a church-steps rally in Rosedale when fearful churches would not open their doors, men jumping from the bushes with rifles to arrest about ten of them and take them overnight to Parchman, the strip search, a jar of liquid that forced everyone to the single toilet, and Morton Thomas of Boston beaten so fiercely by other prisoners that his father sent for him.

title: Jumping on the Burning Rag
topic: A Detroit Doctor and a Gun
summary: Henderson tells of slipping freedom buttons onto her young sister's clothes, doing little on the Meredith March but stopping the car to jump on a burning mosquito rag after a Detroit doctor disparaged the poor people of Mississippi, and her sister handing her a thirty-eight when a car followed them, with instructions to shoot out the back window if it got too close.

title: Right on the Money
summary: She recalls hearing Willie Ricks and Stokely Carmichael in Greenwood after having been at the Nashville meeting where Carmichael was elected, thinking black power necessary and being surprised it turned so many people off, the Deacons for Defense walking armed on the Meredith March, and learning while in school that Benjamin Brown had been killed by police, which briefly broke her.

title: We Had Better Mobilize Again
summary: Henderson grieves Benjamin Brown killed for fetching a sandwich near Jackson State, reflects on the pain of watching the present and the loss of the once close-knit community now scattered, mentions a nephew who is both a Batesville deputy and a preacher and her habit of giving children books so they know where they came from, and closes urging young people to mobilize again because you cannot let it stop you.
